sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=28).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 109, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: MOUSE on palette blocks does nothing. Only UP/DOWN/ACTION7 change gameplay. Agent stuck at cols 11-16. Need to understand initial state and what changed.
- Plan: Check initial frame to understand starting conditions, then try moving agent to each block's row in palette order.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 108 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key findings from comparing initial vs current:
- Initial agent: rows 36-41, cols 11-16 (at bottom of panel)
- Current agent: rows 12-17, cols 11-16 (at top of panel)
- Initial panel background: c (charcoal) 840 pixels, rows 12-41, cols 17-46
- Current panel background: c (charcoal) 732 pixels, rows 16-41, cols 17-46 (smaller because agent moved up)
- Initial vertical trail segments: G at rows 16-19, 22-25, 28-31 (3 segments)
- Current vertical trail segments: G at rows 22-25, 28-31, 34-37 (3 segments, shifted down)
- Initial horizontal trail: none (agent was at bottom)
- Current horizontal trail: c at rows 12-13, cols 17-46 (2 rows of trail)
So the initial state had the agent at the bottom of the panel (rows 36-41), and I moved it UP to the top (rows 12-17).
The vertical trail segments shifted with the agent.
The horizontal trail appeared when I pressed RIGHT.
Now I understand the game better:
1. Agent starts at bottom of panel (rows 36-41)
2. Agent can move UP/DOWN
3. RIGHT/LEFT extend/retract horizontal trail (HUD)
4. ACTION7 retracts trail
5. Vertical trail segments follow the agent
The blocks are at:
- Red: rows 19-22
- Blue: rows 25-28
- Green: rows 31-34
The palette shows: red, green, blue (in that order)
The panel shows: red, blue, green (from top to bottom)
Maybe the goal is to move the agent to touch each block in the palette's order: red, green, blue.
But the agent starts at the bottom (rows 36-41), which is below the green block (rows 31-34).
If I move the agent UP, it would pass the green block first, then the blue block, then the red block.
But the palette order is red, green, blue. So maybe I need to move the agent to the red block first, then the green block, then the blue block.
Wait, but the agent starts at the bottom. To reach the red block (rows 19-22), I need to move UP.
To reach the green block (rows 31-34), I need to move DOWN from the red block.
To reach the blue block (rows 25-28), I need to move UP from the green block.
But this seems complicated. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the goal is to have the agent "collect" the blocks by touching them in the palette's order.
When the agent touches a block, the block disappears or changes color.
Let me check if the blocks have changed since the initial state.
Initial blocks: R at 19-22, b at 25-28, N at 31-34
Current blocks: R at 19-22, b at 25-28, N at 31-34
The blocks are still there, unchanged.
So the agent hasn't "collected" any blocks yet. Maybe I need to move the agent to touch the blocks.
But the agent is at rows 12-17, and the red block is at rows 19-22. The agent is above the red block.
If I move the agent DOWN, it would touch the red block.
Let me try moving the agent DOWN to touch the red block.
Actually wait - the agent is at rows 12-17, cols 11-16. The red block is at rows 19-22, cols 42-45.
The agent and the red block are at different columns. The agent is at cols 11-16, and the red block is at cols 42-45.
So the agent can't touch the red block directly because they're at different columns.
Hmm, but the trail extends from the agent to the right. The trail is at cols 17-46 (when extended).
When the trail overlaps the red block, maybe the block gets "collected".
Let me try: move agent to red block's row, extend trail to overlap red block, then press ACTION7.
Actually, I already tried this. The trail overlapped the red block, but ACTION7 just retracted the trail. The block didn't change.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not to collect the blocks, but to arrange them in the correct order.
The palette shows: red, green, blue.
The panel shows: red, blue, green.
Maybe I need to swap the blue and green blocks.
But how? The blocks are fixed in position. I can't move them.
Unless... the trail can be used to "paint" the blocks. If I extend the trail to overlap a block, the block's color changes to the trail's color.
But the trail's color is gray (g/w), not red, green, or blue.
OK, I'm really stuck. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is to move the agent to a specific position that matches the palette.
The palette shows: agent - w/g - red - w/g - green - w/g - blue
This is a horizontal sequence. The agent is at the left, followed by trail, red, trail, green, trail, blue.
On the panel, the agent is at the left (cols 11-16), and the blocks are at the right (cols 42-45).
The trail extends from the agent to the right.
Maybe the goal is to have the trail overlap all three blocks simultaneously.
But the trail is horizontal, and the blocks are vertical. The trail can only overlap one block at a time.
Unless... the trail can be vertical. Looking at the image, there's a vertical trail at cols 13-14.
The vertical trail extends from the agent DOWN.
If I move the agent to the top of the panel, the vertical trail would extend DOWN, passing all three blocks.
But the vertical trail is at cols 13-14, and the blocks are at cols 42-45. They're at different columns.
Wait, maybe the vertical trail can be moved horizontally. But the agent can only move UP/DOWN.
Hmm, let me check if the agent can move LEFT/RIGHT.
Earlier, I tried RIGHT 10 times, and the agent didn't move (gameplay_changed=False).
But maybe the agent CAN move LEFT/RIGHT, but only when the trail is not extended.
Let me try: ACTION7 (retract trail), then RIGHT.
Actually, let me re-examine the initial state. The agent was at rows 36-41, cols 11-16.
The horizontal trail was not present (the agent was at the bottom, and the trail extends to the right).
Wait, but the initial state had a horizontal trail? Let me check.
Looking at the initial segmentation, there's no horizontal trail node. The panel background is c (charcoal) at rows 12-41, cols 17-46.
So the initial state did NOT have a horizontal trail. The horizontal trail appeared when I pressed RIGHT.
OK so let me try: ACTION7 to retract the trail, then RIGHT to see if the agent moves.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Agent started at bottom (rows 36-41), moved to top (rows 12-17). Agent at cols 11-16, blocks at cols 42-45. Agent can't reach blocks directly.
Plan: Try ACTION7 to retract trail, then RIGHT to see if agent can move horizontally when trail is retracted.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First retract trail with ACTION7
result1 = action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"ACTION7: gameplay_changed={result1.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result1.get('board_changed')}")

# Now try RIGHT to see if agent moves
result2 = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT: gameplay_changed={result2.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result2.get('board_changed')}, level_completed={result2.get('level_completed')}")

# Check agent position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Agent: rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}, cols {min(p[1] for p in b)}-{max(p[1] for p in b)}")

# Check if horizontal trail exists
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('w', 'g', 'c') and n['pixels'] > 20:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        if r_max - r_min < 5 and c_max - c_min > 10:  # Horizontal
            print(f"Horizontal trail: {n['color']} rows {r_min}-{r_max} cols {c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
ACTION7: gameplay_changed=True, board_changed=True
RIGHT: gameplay_changed=True, board_changed=True, level_completed=False
Agent: rows 12-17, cols 11-16
Agent: rows 56-61, cols 20-25
Horizontal trail: c rows 12-13 cols 17-46
Horizontal trail: g rows 53-53 cols 0-34

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First retract trail with ACTION7
result1 = action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"ACTION7: gameplay_changed={result1.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result1.get('board_changed')}")

print(f"RIGHT: gameplay_changed={result2.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result2.get('board_changed')}, level_completed={result2.get('level_completed')}")

# Check agent position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Agent: rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}, cols {min(p[1] for p in b)}-{max(p[1] for p in b)}")

# Check if horizontal trail exists
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('w', 'g', 'c') and n['pixels'] > 20:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        if r_max - r_min < 5 and c_max - c_min > 10:  # Horizontal
            print(f"Horizontal trail: {n['color']} rows {r_min}-{r_max} cols {c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
